text.title: Strangler-Fig Migration Playbook Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for vendor-delivered strangler-fig migration. Violation produces outages, rollback storms, or contract disputes.

rule.statement: Score every monolith route/job/queue on coupling depth (1-5), traffic risk (1-5), business priority (1-5). Start with slices where coupling_depth ≤ 2 AND traffic_risk ≤ 3 AND business_priority ≥ 3. Never start with the "highest value" slice if it has coupling_depth ≥ 4.

rationale.source: Sam Newman, Monolith to Microservices (2019), ch. 3; Fowler StranglerFigApplication

rule.rationale: First slice must build the rails: routing facade, observability parity, kill-switch tooling. A high-coupling first slice spends weeks fighting infrastructure debt instead of validating the pattern. Newman's case studies show first-slice failures kill 60% of migrations within 4 months.

rule.statement: Before retiring a legacy code path, the new service must run in production at the slice's full traffic for ≥7 days with error rate and p99 latency within ±10% of legacy baseline. Skipping the burn-in is the single largest cause of strangler-fig rollbacks.

rationale.source: GitHub API sharded-replicated migration retrospective (2020); Shopify modular monolith program

rule.rationale: Production has edge cases dev/staging don't surface (header anomalies, rare locales, race conditions under real load). 7 days covers a full traffic cycle including peak; ±10% allows for the genuine differences between architectures while flagging regressions. Shopify's strangler-fig public retros explicitly cite 7-14 day burn-in as the gate.

rule.statement: Every slice has a kill-switch (feature flag, routing rule, percentage rollback) that reverts traffic to the legacy path in ≤ 5 minutes by a human with documented commands. Kill-switch revert path is TESTED end-to-end before cutover.

rationale.source: Charity Majors / honeycomb operational practice; Google SRE chapter on rollback

rule.rationale: Strangler-fig assumes you can roll back. A theoretical kill-switch is not a kill-switch. The kill-switch-validate.py script must show a successful revert in staging or a percentage rollback in prod before the slice goes live. ≤ 5 min target keeps the blast radius within MTTR norms.

rule.statement: Dual-write (writes go to both legacy and new) is enabled only during the burn-in window. After burn-in, switch to single-write on new + shadow-read on legacy for ≥7 more days, then retire legacy writes. Dual-write left on permanently creates state divergence bugs that are silent until a year later.

rationale.source: Stripe internal migration practice; AWS prescriptive guidance on data migration patterns

rule.rationale: Dual-write is for safety during cutover, not for the long term. Permanent dual-write hides asymmetric failures (legacy write succeeded, new write failed) and creates two sources of truth. The shadow-read phase catches read-path bugs without write coupling.

rule.statement: Per-slice exit criteria are written into the vendor-client SOW: (a) legacy route returns 5xx for 7 consecutive days, (b) new service maintains parity error rate, (c) runbook handoff signed by client architect. Slice is not "done" until all three are checked.

rationale.source: Statement-of-work / change-order discipline; ThoughtWorks technology radar

rule.rationale: Vendor delivery without contractual exit criteria turns into "is it really done?" debates that delay payment. SOW-anchored criteria align both parties on the same definition of complete and protect against scope creep ("can you also migrate this one too?").

example.title: First slice selection from a 60-route monolith
example.code:
Route scoring (sample): GET /api/products/recommendations coupling=2 traffic_risk=2 priority=4 → SELECT POST /api/checkout coupling=5 traffic_risk=5 priority=5 → DEFER GET /api/health coupling=1 traffic_risk=1 priority=1 → SELECT (rails) POST /webhooks/stripe coupling=4 traffic_risk=4 priority=4 → DEFER
First-3 slice selection: 1. GET /api/health — proves routing facade + observability 2. GET /api/products/recs — proves dual-write/shadow-read pattern on real traffic 3. GET /api/users/profile — proves DB extraction via ACL
Slice 4-6 picked after slice 1-3 burn-in completes.
example.note: Illustrates r1: low-coupling slices first build the rails; high-value high-coupling slices wait until pattern proven.

example.title: Big-slice-first trap
example.code:
Sprint 1 goal: migrate /api/checkout (business-critical, $$$/sec) "to prove value to the client".
Problem: violates r1 (coupling=5, traffic_risk=5). Sprint 1-4 spent fighting session-state coupling, payment gateway timeouts, fraud-detection plugins. By month 3 vendor is over budget, client loses confidence, migration paused. Repair: replan with the low-coupling rails-first slices. Save /api/checkout for slice 5+ after the migration platform is proven.
example.note: The big-slice-first pattern is the #1 vendor-killer. Newman's book opens with this anti-pattern.
